root \ about \ projects + writings \ academic website

introduction

The goal of inferential statistics

Our story begins with the assertion that:

Neither perfect precision nor absolute certainy exist, in all cases, in the real world.

No deep philosophical meaning is intended with this comment; rather, I’m just highlighting the obvious fact that in the real world we often only have access to incomplete information or limited data. Nevertheless, in such situations we often still need to make decisions, predictions, or inferences.

As a very simple example, suppose that we would like to determine the average height of the current population of planet earth. One way to do this is to conduct a planet-wide census of every living person and record their height. We would then have all the data we need in order to find the exact average height.

But clearly such a census would never actually be conducted due to the enormous(!) cost, among many other reasons. Rather, we might instead proceed by taking a small-ish sample of the population:

We would then use the average height of the individuals in the sample as a proxy or estimate for the true average height of the population. And as long as our sample is chosen “randomly,” we would expect that our sample average is likely close to the population average.

Here are some other examples along these same lines:

  1. We estimate the median GPA of all incoming freshmen at SUNY Oswego based on a sample of incoming freshmen.

  2. We estimate the likelihood that a sports team with a given record wins a championship based on the winning records of past champions.

  3. We estimate the likelihood that a tumor of a certain size is malignant or benign based on the diagnoses of past tumors and their sizes.

  4. We predict the next word in a new sentence fragment based on a sample of similar, complete sentences. (This is, in essence, the goal of so-called language models like ChatGPT.)

  5. We estimate the true speed of light based on a sample of measurements obtained in a laboratory.

The single characteristic that each of these examples share is the goal of making an inference or prediction about a population based on a sample. Note that, here, I’m using the word population in a broader sense compared to its typical usage in everyday language. Indeed, the populations in these examples might be the following:

  1. All incoming freshmen.

  2. All win-loss records.

  3. All tumor sizes.

  4. All words.

  5. All speeds.

In particular, notice that a population in statistics need not consist of a population of people or animals, as it often does in everyday language. In fact:

Definition. The population in a given scenario is the collection of items or objects under consideration.

Good! Now that we have identified the populations in our five examples, let’s now identify precisely what features or characteristics of each of the populations we aim to predict or estimate:

  1. The median GPA of the population. (A single number.)

  2. A probability for each win-loss record. (A collection of numbers.)

  3. A probability for each tumor size. (A collection of numbers.)

  4. A probability for each word. (A collection of numbers.)

  5. The identity of the single “true” speed in the population. (A single number.)

Technically, in examples (2.)-(4.), we are attempting to estimate an entire probability distribution, which are the most important objects in this course. We will talk about them in much more detail later.

As you have undoubtably already noticed, the words infer, predict, and estimate are often used interchangeably. Keep this in mind, when you read:

Definition. In a statistical inference task, a feature of a population that we aim to predict or estimate is called a population parameter or population characteristic.

Using our new language, we may state:

The goal of inferential statistics is to estimate population parameters and characteristics based on samples and datasets.

The process of collecting data is called sampling, which passes from the population to the sample. You should then conceptualize inference as a process that goes in the opposite direction:

Naturally, a good part of inferential statistics is devoted to cooking up clever methods of constructing estimates and studying their properties. You should conceptualize these methods as “machines” that take datasets as input, and spit out estimates for whatever population parameter or characteristic the machine is built for. Such machines are called estimators:

For some estimators, you can write down simple formulas that completely describe them. Others might be much more complicated, and are better conceptualized as multistep algorithms. If your “machine” (i.e., estimator) is capable of “learning” how to make good estimates all on its own, then you’ve entered the world of machine learning, a very hot topic these days.

Why probability?

Now that we know a little about the goal of inferential statistics and the role of estimators, I can state one of the main roles that probability theory plays in statistics:

Probability theory gives us the tools to quantify our confidence in our estimators.

Let me give you a specific example of how probability theory is used, using an example from the previous section. It’s not important that you completely and thoroughly understand everything I’m about to say—just focus on getting the broad gist, and rest assured that we’ll talk about this stuff in much more detail later in the course.

Let’s talk about the speed of light!

First, you should know that it is very, very fast—on the order of 300,000 km/s, in fact. This is so incredibly fast that a beam of light would circle the earth about 7.5 times per second:

In 1897, American physicist Albert Michelson set out to improve upon the best estimate of the speed of light at that time. Here is his data:

There are 100 total data points, and to save us from having to write a bunch of extra numbers, each of these data points is equal to

\begin{equation}\notag \text{(estimated speed)} - 299,000. \end{equation}

So, if you want to recover the estimates of the speed of light from Michelson’s data in the format above, you need to add 299,000 to each value. Remember, you should conceptualize Michelson’s dataset as a sample from the larger population (in the technical sense) of all possible speeds:

We then imagine that somewhere in the population of speeds is the “true” speed of light.

This population of speeds is really the open interval $(0,\infty)$ since (theoretically) Michelson could have obtained any positive number, though some numbers—like an estimate of 5 km/s for the speed of light—are far less likely to be obtained than others. If we take advantage of the linear ordering of $(0,\infty)$, we can visualize Michelson’s dataset as:

The horizontal axis is the “speed” axis, while the vertical axis counts how many times a certain speed appears in Michelson’s dataset.

Now, in order to estimate the “true” speed of light, we need an estimator—we need some sort of machine that will take Michelson’s data and spit out an estimate for the “true” speed of light. But there’s an obvious choice for such an estimator, right? The sample average! If we compute the mean value of Michelson’s data, we get a value of

\begin{equation}\notag \bar{x} \defeq 852.4, \end{equation}

which we will take as our official estimate for the “true” speed of light. But how confident are we in the quality of this estimate? If Michelson were to take all the measurements again, obtaining another 100-sample dataset, then our estimate $\bar{x}$ would be different. This might be due to systematic errors in Michelson’s experimental setup, but it might also be due to just plain random error.

So, since the dataset has variability, so too does our estimate $\bar{x}$. Therefore, one of the ways that we might quantify our confidence in the value $852.4$ for $\bar{x}$ is to (attempt to) quantify this variability.

One way to do this is to imagine that it is somehow possible to repeat Michelson’s experiment an infinite number of times, collecting new 100-sample datasets each time, under the exact same conditions as the original experiment. Of course, this is purely a thought exercise, because this isn’t actually possible. Nevertheless, as we go along, we imagine compute the means of each of the new datasets; these means will not all be the same due to the variability that I mentioned above. In the “limit,” we obtain a distribution of mean values that looks roughly something like this:

Along the horizontal axis you see the speeds, as with the plot we saw previously. However, along the vertical axis you now see “probability density,” instead of count values. This just means that the area under the curve (calculus!) represents probability, and that regions along the horizontal axis underneath large amounts of area are the values that are more likely to be observed as means in our infinite sequence of datasets.

Now, a measure of the variability of our estimator could be the interval of values along the horizontal axis over which 95% of the probability ($=$ area!) sits. It is possible to have a computer find these values for us; they are:

\begin{equation}\notag 836.795 \quad \text{and} \quad 867.31. \end{equation}

If we put these two values on the plot above, we get:

So, since 95% of the area under the curve sits between the two dashed red lines, this means that we expect our estimator to output values in the interval $[836.795, 867.31]$ with 95% probability. The length of this interval is about 30 km/s, and since we are dealing with numbers on the order of 300,000 km/s, that length is one part in 10,000 relative to the order of magnitude of the data! Therefore, we may conclude with high confidence that the variability in our estimator is quite small.

Before finishing up this section, let me preview a few more pieces of terminology:

The plots that we saw above are examples of something called a sampling distribution of a sample statistic, which we will study thoroughly toward the end of this course.

Actually, I just lied.

The plots above are really approximations to the true sampling distribution, generated from Michelson’s dataset using a resampling method called bootstrapping. Accordingly, the interval of speeds that we computed is called a bootstrap 95%-confidence interval. We might talk about resampling in MAT 354, but more likely we will cover it next semester in 454.
